title: Inik Kim
text:
I am a Ph.D. student in the Department of Psychology at the University of California, Riverside, working in the Cognitive Neuroscience of Perception, Attention, and Memory Lab under the supervision of Dr. Weiwei Zhang.
My research broadly focuses on visual working memory, attention, and perception. I am particularly interested in understanding the underlying processes of human cognition and behavior, such as memory using cognitive modeling and advanced data analysis techniques.
Before joining UCR, I completed my B.A. and M.A. in Psychology at Jeonbuk National University (South Korea) , where I studied affective neuroscience and psychophysics with Dr. Jongwan Kim and Dr. Chang-Ho Park.

Kim, I., & Park, C. (2022). The influence of perceptual load on target identification and negative repetition effect in post-cueing forced choice task. Korean Journal of Cognitive Science, 33(1), 1–22.

Key Findings: Three experiments examined whether perceptual load modulates the Negative Repetition Effect (NRE) — the accuracy cost when a briefly exposed target shares the same identity as its flanker — using a post-cueing forced-choice task. Experiment 1 found a trend toward NRE but no effect of load (manipulated via flanker similarity). Experiment 2 found a significant NRE and a load effect on overall accuracy, but crucially no interaction between load and NRE. Experiment 3 reinforced load demands using positional variability, yet again found no load effect. Together, the results suggest that the divided-attention demands of the post-cueing task restrict perceptual load’s influence on early perceptual processing, challenging a straightforward extension of Load Theory to this paradigm.
